Software versions

Python version: 3.10.14
Bokeh version: 3.6.1
IPython version: 8.29.0


### Browser name and version

ChromeDriver 131.0.6778.69

### Jupyter notebook / Jupyter Lab version

_No response_

### Expected behavior

The Div text should be shown in the exported SVG file and no random temporary files should be created!

### Observed behavior

Exporting Bokeh layout or gridplot to SVG does not show Div elements. Moreover, the code generates a new temporary `html` files (with a random name) in the working directory every time the code is executed even when I have specified an `output_file` name. If `output_file` name is specified, then the temporary file creation is skipped at the first execution. 

### Example code

```Python
import numpy as np

from bokeh.plotting import figure, output_file
from bokeh.models import Div
import bokeh.layouts as layouts
from bokeh.io import export_svg

N = 10000

x = np.random.normal(0, np.pi, N)
y = np.sin(x) + np.random.normal(0, 0.2, N)

TOOLS = "pan,wheel_zoom,box_zoom,reset,save,box_select"

p = figure(tools=TOOLS, output_backend="svg")
p.scatter(x, y, alpha=0.1, nonselection_alpha=0.001)

div = Div(text='Plotting random')

fig = layouts.column(div, p)

output_file('testSVG.html')

export_svg(fig, filename='testSVG.svg')
```

discussion","color":"2659A0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2020-09-15T07:29:04Z","updated_at":"2024-11-19T13:27:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi folks!

I’m trying to export some figures to PNGs with bokeh.io.export_png(fig, filename=fname, width=width, height=height).

And I’m getting some strange behaviour: when I call this function second time, the next image is 100px wider than I ordered by width argument. Third image is 200px wider — and so on.

Here’s a small code example:

```
import numpy as np
import bokeh.io
from bokeh.plotting import *
from bokeh.layouts import column, gridplot, layout
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # lets' prepare some date
    N = 300
    x = np.linspace(0, 4 * np.pi, N)
    y0 = np.sin(x)
    y1 = np.cos(x)
    y2 = np.abs(np.cos(x))

    # create a column data source for the plots to share
    source = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(x=x, y0=y0, y1=y1, y2=y2))

    # create a new plot and add a renderer
    left = figure(width=600, height=400, title=None)
    left.circle('x', 'y0', source=source)

    right = figure(width=600, height=350, title=None)
    right.circle('x', 'y1', source=source)

    bottom = figure(width=600, height=400, title=None)
    bottom.circle('x', 'y2', source=source)

    # put the subplots in a gridplot
    layout = layout([[left, right], [bottom]], sizing_mode='stretch_width', height=1800)

    show(layout)  # show the plots in layout: layout looks okay...

    # And let's try to export some figures to PNGs (here we export only two images, but for real tasks we have more
    # complicated layouts, and we use bokeh server to modify plots on-the-fly and export them again and again).

    for i in range(5):
        bokeh.io.export_png(left, filename="left{}.png".format(i), width=800, height=500)
        bokeh.io.export_png(right, filename="right{}.png".format(i), width=800, height=500)

    # And what we get? After each export to PNG each next image becomes 100px wider. I don't know why = (((

    # left0.png: 800x500
    # right0.png: 900x500
    # left1.png: 1000x500
    # right1.png: 1100x500
    # left2.png: 1200x500
    # right2.png: 1300x500
    # left3.png: 1400x500
    # right3.png: 1500x500
    # left4.png: 1600x500
    # left4.png: 1700x500, it's almost twice wider than ordered!
```

If this can be fixed -- it will be really cool, because we use bokeh to have a dynamic dashboard with bokeh server and it's a really important feature for us to be capable to save the plots each 100-150 steps (about 5 minutes).

P.S. I use bokeh version 2.2.1 (installed by `pip install --force bokeh`), OS: Windows 10x64, webdriver: chrome","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/10496/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/10496/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2485714420","html_url":"https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/10496#issuecomment-2485714420","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/10496","id":2485714420,"node_id":"IC_kwDOADqB3M6UKP30","user":{"login":"PrateekArya","id":1255663,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyNTU2NjM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1255663?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya","html_url":"https://github.com/PrateekArya","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/PrateekArya/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-19T13:27:44Z","updated_at":"2024-11-19T13:27:44Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Never mind I found an easy fix. 😉 I do this when I am exporting the figure layout to PNG.

`export_png(fig.clone(sizing_mode=None), filename='fileName.png')`","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/2485714420/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> 
> ```
> TOOLS="crosshair,pan,wheel_zoom,box_zoom,undo,redo,reset,tap,save,box_select,poly_select,lasso_select"
> 
> p = figure(tools=TOOLS, active_tap="pan")
> ```

Hello!

I am using this approach to set `box_zoom` as active for a grid plot with multiple `figure`. It works, but there are 2 things which make me think that I am not using it the correct way:

1. I get the following warning:

`....py:208: UserWarning: found multiple competing values for 'toolbar.active_drag' property; using the latest value`

2. The box zoom icon in the tool box is not highlighted, but it is active!

Creating this request as a follow up from this discourse: [link](https://discourse.bokeh.org/t/adding-decoration-to-multiline/11772?u=prateekarya)

I am trying to add decorations to `multi_line`, specifically arrowheads. Here is the code snippet:

```
from bokeh.models import VeeHead
ml = p.multi_line(xs='xs', ys='ys', color='black', source=dataSource)
ml.add_decoration(VeeHead(size=10), "end")
```

The code does not throw any exceptions, but it does not draw arrowheads. So, I am guessing the functionality exists but it's not implemented yet.

### Feature description

Enable decorating `multi_line` with Vee and Tee heads.

### Potential alternatives

Use more `multi_line` to draw these decoations.

### Additional information
